node_2: large amounts of manual data annotation
edge: Action recognition has achieved remarkable progress but relies heavily on large amounts of manual data annotation, limiting scalability to unseen categories.
Few-shot action recognition is a promising direction aiming to identify novel classes with extremely limited labeled videos.

Mainstream few-shot action recognition approaches adopt the metric-based meta-learning strategy to map videos into an appropriate feature space and then perform alignment metrics to predict query labels.
OTAM leverages a deep network to extract video features and estimates an ordered temporal alignment path to match the frames of two videos.
HyRSM proposes to explore task-specific semantic correlations across videos to design a bidirectional approach.

HyRSM designs a bidirectional Mean Hausdorff Metric (Bi-MHM) to align frames.
Bi-MHM is designed to align frames in videos.
Local frame features can predict the global context in few-shot classification.
Motion dynamics are widely regarded as a vital role in the field of video understanding.
Existing few-shot methods do not explore the rich motion cues between frames for the matching procedure.
Existing few-shot methods result in a sub-optimal performance due to not exploring rich motion cues.
Traditional action recognition works introduce motion information by feeding optical flow or frame difference into an additional deep network.
An efficient motion compensation method should be introduced to achieve comprehensive few-shot matching.
MoLo is a motion-augmented long-short contrastive learning method developed based on the above observations.
MoLo method is inspired by long-short contrastive learning to jointly model global contextual information and motion dynamics.
The global context is explicitly integrated into the local matching process using a long-short contrastive objective.
Frame features are enforced to predict the global context of videos that belong to the same class.
A motion autodecoder is used to explicitly extract motion features between frame representations by reconstructing pixel motions.
MoLo enables the exploitation of temporal contextual dependencies and motion cues for accurate few-shot action recognition.
Experimental results demonstrate that MoLo outperforms other advanced few-shot techniques and achieves state-of-the-art performance.
The autodecoder is designed to explicitly extract motion cues.
The results of the experiments demonstrate that MoLo significantly outperforms baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance.

edge: Few-shot learning is identified as an important research direction in computer vision.
Few-shot learning pertains to identifying unseen classes using only a few labels.
Few-shot learning is closely related to few-shot image classification as described in this paper.
Existing few-shot learning techniques are discussed in relation to the research in few-shot learning.
Existing few-shot learning techniques can generally be divided into three groups: augmentation-based, gradient optimization, and metric-based methods.
Augmentation-based approaches usually learn to generate samples to alleviate the data scarcity dilemma.
Augmentation-based approaches usually learn to generate samples, including hallucinating additional training examples and adversarial generation.
Gradient optimization methods attempt to modify the network optimization process so that the model can be quickly fine-tuned to near the optimal point.
